content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob are competing in the finals of a FightBots tournament.

The match takes place on the 222-D plane.

Alice's robot starts at position (0,0)(0, 0)(0,0), while Bob's starts at position (X,Y)(X, Y)(X,Y).

It is guaranteed that (X,Y)≠(0,0)(X, Y) \neq (0, 0)(X,Y)=(0,0).

The match lasts for NNN seconds. Every second, both robots must move exactly one unit up, down, left, or right. The two robots may move in different directions.

Alice has a string SSS of length NNN containing only the characters {L,R,U,D}\{\texttt{L,R,U,D}\}{L,R,U,D}, and has decided that she will move according to it.

That is, if Si=RS_i = \texttt{R}Si​=R, Alice will move her robot one unit right in the iii-th second. **This means the xxx-coordinate of Alice's robot will increase by 111.**

Similarly, the characters L, U, D\texttt{L, U, D}L, U, D correspond to left (**xxx-coordinate decreases by 111**), up (**yyy-coordinate increases by 111**), and down (**yyy-coordinate decreases by 111**) movements of one unit, respectively.

If both robots are at the same location after some moves have been made, Bob wins (even if less than NNN seconds have passed). If NNN seconds pass without this happening, Alice wins.

Does Bob have a sequence of moves that will allow him to win?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains three space-separated integers N,X,N, X, N,X, and YYY — the length of the match, and the coordinates of Bob's initial position.
The second line contains a string SSS of length NNN, denoting the sequence of moves Alice will make.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: `Yes` if Bob has a sequence of moves that will allow him to win, and `No` otherwise.


Each character of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, the strings `NO`, `no`, `No`, and `nO` will all be treated as equivalent.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Alice's sequence of moves is RRDLU\texttt{RRDLU}RRDLU.
Her robot will thus follow the path (0,0)→(1,0)→(2,0)→(2,−1)→(1,−1)→(1,0)(0,0)\to (1,0)\to (2,0)\to (2,-1)\to (1,-1) \to (1,0)(0,0)→(1,0)→(2,0)→(2,−1)→(1,−1)→(1,0).
Bob starts at (2,4)(2, 4)(2,4). If he follows the path DDLDD\texttt{DDLDD}DDLDD, his robot will move as:
(2,4)→(2,3)→(2,2)→(1,2)→(1,1)→(1,0)(2,4)\to (2,3) \to (2,2) \to (1,2) \to (1,1) \to (1,0)(2,4)→(2,3)→(2,2)→(1,2)→(1,1)→(1,0).
At the end of the 555-th move, both robots are at (1,0)(1, 0)(1,0), so Bob wins.
Test case 222: Alice's sequence of moves is RRDU\texttt{RRDU}RRDU.
Her robot will thus follow the path (0,0)→(1,0)→(2,0)→(2,−1)→(2,0)(0,0)\to (1,0)\to (2,0)\to (2,-1)\to (2,0)(0,0)→(1,0)→(2,0)→(2,−1)→(2,0).
Even though Bob's robot starts at (1,0)(1, 0)(1,0), since he must move every second, he can never be on the same position as Alice.
Test case 333: Bob starts too far away from Alice.
Test case 444: Bob can move as LDU\texttt{LDU}LDU. After the second move, both robots will be at (0,0)(0, 0)(0,0) so Bob wins.
